critic_raw:
This multi-task learning experiment for 30-day readmission prediction has several fundamental methodological problems.

1. AUXILIARY TASK SELECTION IS SCIENTIFICALLY UNJUSTIFIED AND CREATES HIDDEN LABEL LEAKAGE. The auxiliary tasks include 90-day readmission, length of stay, and comorbidity patterns. The 90-day readmission outcome is computed from EHR records produced after the index discharge -- the same period that contains the 30-day readmission label. Sharing a representation layer trained jointly on 30-day and 90-day outcomes does not constitute leakage in the strict preprocessing sense, but it does mean the auxiliary task supervision signal encodes information that is correlated with, and temporally downstream of, the primary label. The hypothesis is that multi-task learning improves generalization via shared representation; but if 90-day readmission is the auxiliary task, the model is partially trained on an outcome that post-dates the prediction event, artificially boosting the shared representation quality in ways that would not persist if the 90-day label were unavailable at deployment.

2. THE BASELINE IS NOT A FAIR COMPARISON FOR ISOLATING MULTI-TASK LEARNING. The baseline is logistic regression on structured data only, while the multi-task model adds both (a) clinical notes via LSTM/transformer and (b) multi-task objectives. These are two simultaneous advantages. A meaningful ablation would require a single-task neural model with the same architecture and note features but trained on only the primary task. Without this control, the observed improvement, if any, cannot be attributed to multi-task learning specifically versus note inclusion or neural capacity.

3. TEMPORAL STRATIFICATION WITHIN TEMPORAL SPLITS CONTRADICTS THE DEPLOYMENT SCENARIO. The design uses stratified temporal splits -- within each temporal window, records are stratified by readmission status. This is methodologically contradictory: temporal splitting is justified because readmission rates evolve over time, but then within-window stratification forces the class distribution to be identical across the splits, erasing exactly the temporal drift that motivated the temporal split. If readmission rates genuinely change over 3 years, forcing balanced class distributions across windows creates an artificial evaluation scenario that does not reflect deployment.

4. CLINICAL NOTE ENCODING VIA LSTM OR TRANSFORMER IS NOT PRETRAINED-CONTROLLED. The design mentions using an LSTM or transformer encoder for clinical notes, but does not specify whether this encoder is pretrained on clinical text (e.g., ClinicalBERT) or trained from scratch on the training set. If trained from scratch on 30,000 records, the clinical note encoder is severely underpowered and the multi-task model is evaluated at a disadvantage relative to its potential. If pretrained, the comparison must account for the fact that the baseline logistic regression does not benefit from any pretraining -- the advantage may come from the pretrained encoder, not from multi-task learning.

5. AUROC AS PRIMARY METRIC MASKS CLINICAL DECISION THRESHOLD BEHAVIOR. The design correctly notes that AUROC is threshold-agnostic. But hospital readmission interventions are threshold-dependent -- at deployment, a threshold is chosen and the model operates at a single operating point. AUROC can be inflated by improvements at operating points that will never be used in deployment (e.g., very high recall / very low precision). The calibration error is listed as secondary, but for a clinical risk tool, calibration at the actual deployment threshold is arguably the primary concern, not global discrimination.
all_issues_raised: Auxiliary 90-day readmission task encodes temporally downstream information correlated with the primary label, inflating shared representation quality
Baseline does not isolate multi-task learning benefit from note inclusion or neural architecture advantages
Within-window stratification on readmission rate contradicts the temporal drift motivation for temporal splitting
Clinical note encoder pretraining status is unspecified, confounding the source of any improvement
AUROC as primary metric masks calibration behavior at the clinically relevant deployment threshold
Auxiliary task selection unjustified and not ablated; multi-task mechanism not isolable from capacity or regularization effects
Baseline uses structured features only while proposed model uses structured+text; two confounds conflated in a single comparison
Stratified temporal split imposes artificial class balance inconsistent with real deployment distribution
Validation set reused for hyperparameter search creates selection bias; reported metrics may be optimistically biased
Calibration reported as secondary metric despite being clinically primary for probability-based decision support; calibration degradation from auxiliary losses may be masked
Auxiliary task selection lacks mechanistic justification for why shared representation should improve 30-day readmission specifically
Dual confound between multi-task learning, clinical notes, and neural architecture — no single-task neural note-using baseline
Stratified temporal split creates leakage risk if patients appear in multiple time windows and stratification is not patient-deduplication-aware
Sensitivity/specificity operating point selected post-hoc on test set rather than fixed on validation
Tuning space geometry is radically asymmetric between multi-task model and logistic regression, not truly equalized effort
all_issues_adjudicated: Auxiliary 90-day readmission task encodes temporally downstream information correlated with the primary label, inflating shared representation quality
